instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
When To Opt For A Staggered Payout Option In Term Insurance Plan?
A term insurance plan pays out a death benefit if the insured dies during the policy tenure. The pay-out of the death benefit can be done in multiple ways. The basic pay-out mode is the lump sum mode wherein the sum assured is paid in a lump sum on death. However, modern-day term plans have introduced the staggered pay-out option too. Let’s understand what the option is all about.
What is the staggered payout option?
Under the staggered pay-out option, the death benefit is paid either partly in a lump sum and partly in instalments or in instalments over a specified tenure.
For instance, 50% of the benefit can be paid in a lump sum and the next 50% in the form of instalments. Alternatively, the plan might pay 10% of the benefit in instalments for the next ten years.
The staggered pay-out mode has different pay-out options too that are available. Let’s have a look at the options.
Different types of staggered payout options
Part lump sum part instalment
Under this option, a part of the sum assured is paid in a lump sum immediately after the insured dies. The remaining sum assured is paid in instalments, annually or monthly.
For example, say your sum assured is Rs.50 lakhs and the plan offers 40% of the sum assured in a lump sum and the remaining in monthly instalments over the next five years. In this case, your family would receive Rs.20 lakhs in a lump sum on death. Then, from the next month, they would get Rs.50,000 as monthly income over the next 60 months.
The instalments might also increase after every year if the plan allows.
Monthly incomes
Under this option, no lump sum benefit is paid. Instead, the sum assured is paid in instalments from the start.
For example, if the sum assured of the term plan is Rs.50 lakhs and the plan pays monthly instalments for the next ten years, your family would receive Rs.41,667 as monthly instalments over the next ten years.
Increasing monthly incomes
This option is similar to the previous one, with the only difference being that the instalments increase. Under this mode, the monthly instalments increase every year so that your family gets an increasing income to meet their rising expenses.
For instance, if, in the first year, the instalment is Rs.50,000 every month, it might increase to Rs.55,000 or Rs.60,000 in the next year, depending on the plan’s benefits.
When should you choose the staggered payout option?
A staggered pay-out option makes sense in the following instances –
When your family needs a regular source of income
If your family needs a regular source of income in your absence, the staggered pay-out option can be a suitable choice. The instalment payments would provide the income that your family loses when you are not around.
If there are no immediate financial goals
If you have created sufficient savings for your financial goals and your only concern is your family’s lifestyle expenses, the staggered pay-out option would be suitable. It would give your family the funds needed to meet their daily lifestyle needs while their goals would be met by the savings that you have created additionally.
Alternatively, if your family doesn’t have immediate financial goals that need a lump sum amount, the instalment payment mode can be chosen.
If your family can invest the pay-outs and save for future goals
If your family can utilise the pay-outs and save them to create a corpus for their future financial goals, you can choose such pay-outs. Your family can invest the excess funds into a savings avenue, earn returns on the investment and create a corpus for their goals.
Things to remember
A staggered payment makes sense in the aforementioned scenarios. If, however, your family needs a lump sum fund to take care of their financial goals, the basic lump sum option is ideal.
So, assess your and your family’s needs when selecting the staggered option. Also, keep the following points in mind –
Choose the term over which the payments would be made. The term should match your family’s needs.
Opt for an optimal sum assured
You can choose the increasing instalment option if you are worried about inflation.
If you are choosing the staggered option under the term plan, advise your family to invest the excess funds in a suitable avenue to earn returns.
Conclusion
The staggered payout option can be a good feature in term insurance plans when it comes to the payment of benefits. However, choose the option only if it would fulfil your family’s financial needs in your absence. Also, choose the right pay-out term and ensure that the instalments are sufficient to meet your family's expenses.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale. output.final_text:
When To Opt For A Staggered Payout Option In Term Insurance Plan?
A term insurance plan pays out a death benefit if the insured dies during the policy tenure provided all due premiums are paid. The pay-out of the death benefit can be done in multiple ways. The usual pay-out mode is the lump sum mode wherein the sum assured is paid as lump sum on death. However, modern-day term plans have introduced the staggered pay-out option too. Let’s understand what the option is all about.
What is the staggered payout option?
Under the staggered pay-out option, the death benefit is paid either partly in a lump sum and partly in instalments or in instalments over a specified tenure. The duration of the payments varies from policy to policy.
For instance, 50% of the benefit can be paid in a lump sum and the next 50% in the form of instalments. Alternatively, the plan might pay 10% of the benefit in instalments for the next ten years as per terms and conditions detailed out in the policy document.
The staggered pay-out mode has different pay-out options too that are available. Let’s have a look at the options.
Some types of staggered payout options
1. Part lump sum part instalment
Under this option, a part of the sum assured is paid as lump sum immediately after the insured dies. The remaining sum assured is paid in instalments, annually or monthly or as per payout frequency selected. For example, say your sum assured is Rs. 50 lakhs and the plan offers a pay-out at 40% of the sum assured as lump sum and the remaining in monthly instalments over the next five years. In this case, your family (nominee) would receive Rs.20 lakhs as lump sum on death. Then, from the next month, they would get Rs. 50,000 as monthly income over the next 60 months. 2. Monthly income
Under this option, no lump sum benefit is paid. Instead, the sum assured is paid in instalments post the death of the insured. For example, if the sum assured of the term plan is Rs.50 lakhs with the pay-out in monthly instalments for a period of ten years, your family would receive Rs.41,667 as monthly instalments over a period of ten years on occurrence of the insured event. 3. Increasing monthly income
This option is similar to the previous one, with the only difference being that the instalments increases. Under this mode, the monthly instalments increases every year so that your family gets an increasing income to meet their rising expenses. For instance, if, in the first year, the instalment is Rs. 50,000 every month, it might increase to Rs. 55,000 or Rs. 60,000 in the next year, depending on the plan’s benefits.
When should you choose the staggered pay out option?
A staggered pay-out option makes sense in the following instances –
A. When your family needs a regular source of income
If your family needs a regular source of income in your absence, the staggered pay-out option may be a suitable choice. The instalment payments would provide the income that your family might lose when you are not around. B. If there are no immediate financial goals
If you have created sufficient savings for your financial goals and your only concern is your family’s routine expenses, the staggered pay-out option may be suitable. It might give your family the funds needed to meet their daily lifestyle needs while their goals would be met by the savings that you might have created additionally. Alternatively, if your family doesn’t have immediate financial goals that need a lump sum amount, the instalment payment mode can be chosen. C. If your family can invest the pay-outs and save for future goals
If your family can utilise the staggered pay-outs and save them to create a corpus for their future financial goals, you may choose such pay-outs.
Things to remember
A staggered payment makes sense in the aforementioned scenarios. One of the major reasons around taking staggered pay outs in a term insurance plan is that the family may not be financially literate & capable of handling a huge lump sum inflow of funds and allocating these wisely. A regular stream of income is an apt solution under such circumstances.
If, however, your family needs a lump sum fund to take care of their financial goals or liabilities, the usual lump sum option may be preferred.
So, consider assessing your and your family’s needs while selecting the staggered option. Also, consider following points–
1. Choose the term over which the payments would be made. The term should match your
family’s needs. 2. Opt for a suitable sum assured 3. If you are choosing the staggered option under the term plan, your family may consider
investing the excess funds out of the instalments received in a suitable financial instrument.
Conclusion
The staggered pay-out option can be a suitable feature in term insurance plans when it comes to the payment of benefits. However, consider the option only if it would fulfil your family’s financial needs in your absence. Also, choose the suitable pay-out term and ensure that the instalments are sufficient to meet your family's expenses.
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document
(available on www.bajajallianzlife.com) carefully before concluding a sale.
